revision.text:
{{Shrine |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji}} (鹽土老翁) |rank= {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|setsumatsusha|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). It ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|deity|ja|deity}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
[[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines of Toyouke Daijingu]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa (Mie)|ja|宮川}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|Meiō earthquake|ja|明応地震}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
[[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]

[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|setsumatsusha|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). It ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|deity|ja|deity}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
[[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines of Toyouke Daijingu]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa (Mie)|ja|宮川}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|Meiō earthquake|ja|明応地震}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
[[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]

{{Shrine |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji}} (鹽土老翁) |rank= {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|setsumatsusha|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). It ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|deity|ja|deity}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
[[Category:Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines of Toyouke Daijingu]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa (Mie)|ja|宮川}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|Meiō earthquake|ja|明応地震}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
[[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:志宝屋神社]]

Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|Meiō earthquake|ja|明応地震}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}}

{{Shrine |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji}} (鹽土老翁) |rank= {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|setsumatsusha|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|ja|摂末社}} of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). It ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|deity|ja|deity}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa (Mie)|ja|宮川}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|鹽竈神社}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|Meiō earthquake|ja|明応地震}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}}

7 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:志宝屋神社 up to rev 98063831
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 志宝屋神社 |画像= [[File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[大湊 (伊勢市)|大湊町]] |緯度度= 34 | 緯度分 = 31 | 緯度秒 = 34.4 |経度度= 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 47.1 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= [[シオツチノオジ|鹽土老翁]] |社格= [[豊受大神宮]]末社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前 |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= [[明神]]さん、湊の明神さま、塩屋明神 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} [[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|社叢全景。参道口に鳥居が見える。]] '''志宝屋神社'''（しおやじんじゃ）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の[[摂末社|末社]]。外宮の末社8社のうち第8位である<ref>宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：13, 15ページ</ref>。
[[祭神]]は塩業の[[神 (神道)|神]]であり、鎮座地の[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[大湊 (伊勢市)|大湊町]]では、かつて製塩業が盛んに行われていた<ref name="gk">学研パブリッシング（2013）：63ページ</ref>。
== 概要 == 20世紀までは[[造船]]の街であった伊勢市大湊町に鎮座する<ref name="ic">伊勢文化舎（2008）：46ページ</ref>。大湊町は河口の[[三角州]]であり、周囲を堤防で囲まれている<ref name="ic"/>。伊勢市御薗町方面から[[宮川 (三重県)|宮川]]の堤防道路に沿って進むと、志宝屋神社の森が見える<ref name="ic"/>。神社の森は「鵜の森」と呼ばれる<ref name="gk"/>。神域の面積は2反2畝23歩（約2,257.85[[平方メートル|m<sup>2</sup>]]）<ref name="uy">宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：15ページ</ref>。
周辺住民からは「明神さん」<ref name="km">{{Cite web|和書| url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=造船の町大湊の歴史散歩その1 - ふるさと三重、再発見の旅|work=観光三重| publisher=三重県観光連盟 | accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>、「湊の明神さま」、「塩屋明神」と呼ばれ、親しまれる<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>。
== 祭神 == 祭神は[[シオツチノオジ|鹽土老翁]]（しおつちのおじ）<ref name="ic"/>。塩業の神であるとともに海路の[[守護神]]でもある<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>が、地域住民からは安産の神としても広く信仰されている<ref name="ic"/>。
[[宮城県]][[塩竈市]]の[[鹽竈神社]]より1座の御魂を[[分霊]]して祀ったとする伝承がある<ref name="km"/>。
== 歴史 == 伊勢神宮の末社の定義より『延暦儀式帳』成立、すなわち[[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前に創建された<ref>伊勢文化舎（2008）：22ページ</ref>。鎮座地周辺は「大塩屋村」と呼ばれ、伊勢神宮に[[奉納]]する御塩を焼く村人が多かったという<ref name="km"/>。[[櫻井勝之進]]は、志宝屋神社に関連して、以下のように述べている<ref>櫻井（1970）：93ページ</ref>。 {{Quotation|塩屋というのは後世に大湊として繁栄した港町の古名といわれるが、当時はまだその社も末官帳社であったとすれば、塩焼く煙の立つほどの小村にすぎなかったのであろうか。 |櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』93ページ}}
志宝屋神社の[[社殿]]は[[明応]]7年[[8月25日 (旧暦)|8月25日]]（[[ユリウス暦]]：[[1498年]][[9月11日]]）に発生した[[明応地震]]により、大塩屋村の家々1,000軒とともに流され、失われた<ref name="km"/>。その後、[[寛永]]21年（[[1644年]]）に再興され、今日に至る<ref name="km"/>。<!--社殿は[[1908年]]（明治41年）11月に建て替えられた<ref name="uy"/>。-->
== 交通 == * [[三重交通]][[路線バス|バス]]「徳田」[[バス停留所|バス停]]より徒歩約7分（約600[[メートル|m]]）、「西町」バス停より徒歩約9分（約750m）<!--「Yahoo!ロコ」での測定結果による-->。 * [[国道23号]]（[[南勢バイパス]]）「新開北」[[交差点]]を北上、[[三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線]]経由、約8分（約2.9[[キロメートル|km]]）<!--「Yahoo!ロコ」での測定結果による-->。付近に[[駐車場]]はない。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45年10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4
== 関連項目 == * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] * [[神宮125社の一覧]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m04/0402.html 志宝屋神社（しおやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:しおやしんしや}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:塩竈神社]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]]

{{神社 |名称= 志宝屋神社 |画像= [[File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[大湊 (伊勢市)|大湊町]] |緯度度= 34 | 緯度分 = 31 | 緯度秒 = 34.4 |経度度= 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 47.1 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= [[シオツチノオジ|鹽土老翁]] |社格= [[豊受大神宮]]末社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前 |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= [[明神]]さん、湊の明神さま、塩屋明神 |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} [[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|社叢全景。参道口に鳥居が見える。]] '''志宝屋神社'''（しおやじんじゃ）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の[[摂末社|末社]]。外宮の末社8社のうち第8位である<ref>宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：13, 15ページ</ref>。

周辺住民からは「明神さん」<ref name="km">{{Cite web|和書| url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=造船の町大湊の歴史散歩その1 - ふるさと三重、再発見の旅|work=観光三重| publisher=三重県観光連盟 | accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>、「湊の明神さま」、「塩屋明神」と呼ばれ、親しまれる<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>。

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|qid=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|qid=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|qq=|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|qq=|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|qq=|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m04/0402.html 志宝屋神社（しおやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|qq=|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|qq=|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|qq=|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m04/0402.html 志宝屋神社（しおやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
