<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}

Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m04/0402.html 志宝屋神社（しおやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a [[:en:Setsumatsusha|subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)]] of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa (Mie)]] River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 櫻井勝之進『伊勢神宮』[[学生社]]、昭和45th year 10月20日重版、251p. * 『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、[[伊勢文化舎]]、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}

He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama Shrine]] in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the [[:en:1498 Meiō earthquake|Meiō earthquake]] on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|WD=Q1071160|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)}} of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:志宝屋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211291]] Wikidata name: Shioya Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Shioya Shrine |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Shioya-jinja 03.jpg |location= {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} |latitude_deg= 34 | latitude_min = 31 | latitude_sec = 34.4 |longitude_deg= 136 |longitude_min = 43 | longitude_sec = 47.1 |map= Japan Mie |deity= {{ill|Shiotsuchi|WD=Q11174872}} |Rank= {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Subordinate Shrine |founded= Before [[Enryaku]] 23 (804) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} |other_names= *Myōjin-san* (明神さん), *Minato no Myōjin-sama* (湊の明神さま), *Shioya Myōjin* (塩屋明神) |pilgrimage_sites= |annual_festival= |rituals= {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|en|Kannamesai Festival|WD=Q11255045|lt=Kannamesai}} |prefecture= {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.526222|N|136.72975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|WD=Q1071160|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)}} of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}

Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.

He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.

The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}

It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.

There are no parking facilities nearby.

'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|WD=Q1071160|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)}} of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
== References == {{Reflist}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}}

'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|WD=Q1071160|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|1=Setsumatsusha}} of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)|1=Miyagawa}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine|1=Shiogama shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake|1=1498 Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu|1=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)|1=Miyagawa}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine|1=Shiogama shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake|1=1498 Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu|1=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.

[[File:Shioya-jinja 04.jpg|thumb|Panoramic view of the shrine grove with a torii gate visible at the entrance.]]
'''Shioya Shrine''' (志宝屋神社, *Shioya Jinja*) is a {{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|WD=Q1071160|lt=subordinate shrine (sessha/massha)|1=Setsumatsusha}} of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}). It Ranks 8th among the eight subordinate shrines of the Outer Shrine<ref>Ujiyamada City Office, ed. (1929): pp.13, 15</ref>.
The enshrined {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} is the god of salt production. In its location at {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|1=Ominato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437524}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, salt production was once a flourishing industry<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.63</ref>.
== Overview == {{ill|Shiho-ya Shrine|ja|志宝屋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiho-ya Shrine|1=Shioya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17211291}} is located in Ōminato Town, Ise City, which was known as a shipbuilding town until the 20th century<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.46</ref>. Ōminato Town is situated on a river delta surrounded by levees<ref name="ic"/>. Approaching from the Misato Town area of Ise City along the levee road of the {{ill|Miyagawa|en|Miyagawa|WD=Q1665818|lt=Miyagawa (Mie)|1=Miyagawa}} River, one can see the forest of Shiho-ya Shrine<ref name="ic"/>. This sacred forest is referred to as the "Forest of Cormorants" (鵜の森)<ref name="gk"/>. The shrine grounds cover an area of approximately 2,257.85 m²<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p.15</ref>.
Locally, the shrine is affectionately called "Myōjin-san" (明神さん)<ref name="km">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|title=A Historical Walk in the Shipbuilding Town of Ōminato Part 1|work=Visit Mie|publisher=Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation|accessdate=2013-09-29|archiveurl=https://megalodon.jp/2013-0929-0920-23/www.kankomie.or.jp/kataribe/course_11725.html|archivedate=2013-09-29}}</ref>, "Minato no Myōjin-sama" (湊の明神さま), and "Shioya Myōjin" (塩屋明神)<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>.
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is '''Shiotsuchi-no-Oji''' (鹽土老翁)<ref name="ic"/>. He is revered as the god of salt production as well as the guardian deity of maritime routes<ref name="gk"/><ref name="ic"/>. Additionally, he is widely worshiped by local residents as a deity of safe childbirth<ref name="ic"/>.
There is a tradition that a sacred spirit (mitama) was transferred (bunrei) from {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama Shrine|1=Shiogama shrine}} in Shiogama City, Miyagi Prefecture<ref name="km"/>.
== History == Based on the definition of auxiliary shrines of the Ise Grand Shrine, Shiho-ya Shrine was established before the compilation of the ''Enryaku Gishiki-chō'', that is, before 804 (Enryaku 23)<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.22</ref>. The area surrounding the shrine was known as "Ōshioya Village," where many villagers were engaged in salt production for offerings to the Ise Grand Shrine<ref name="km"/>. Regarding Shiho-ya Shrine, {{ill|Sakurai Katsunosuke|ja|櫻井勝之進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Katsunosuke|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}} remarked<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.93</ref>: {{Quotation|Although 'Shioya' is said to be the old name of what later flourished as the port town of Ōminato, if the shrine was still considered a subordinate shrine at the time, perhaps it was just a small village with smoke rising from salt kilns. |Sakurai Katsunosuke, ''Ise Jingū'', p.93}}
The shrine building was destroyed along with 1,000 homes in Ōshioya Village during the {{ill|1498 Meiō earthquake|en|1498 Meiō earthquake|WD=Q4549898|lt=Meiō earthquake|1=1498 Meiō earthquake}} on August 25, 1498 (Julian calendar: September 11, 1498)<ref name="km"/>. It was later rebuilt in 1644 (Kan'ei 21) and has survived to the present day<ref name="km"/>.
== Access == * By Bus: About a 7-minute walk (approximately 600 m) from the "Tokuda" bus stop or about a 9-minute walk (approximately 750 m) from the "Nishimachi" bus stop serviced by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu|1=Mie Kotsu}} buses. * By Car: From the "Shinkai Kita" intersection on National Route 23 (Nansei Bypass), head north via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 748|ja|三重県道748号大湊宮町停車場線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 748|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 748|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358136}}, approximately 8 minutes (about 2.9 km). There are no parking facilities nearby.
